title: SriLankan Airlines Restructuring Stalls as Losses Return in 2025
dek: SriLankan Airlines' government-led restructuring faced delays by late 2025 as the carrier swung back to a loss for the year ending March 2025, reviving debate over the national airline's privatization.
body:
SriLankan Airlines' long-running, government-led restructuring stalled as 2025 wore on, with the national carrier swinging back into the red for the financial year ending March 2025 after a rare prior-year profit. The reversal reignited debate over the airline's future ownership and the credibility of a turnaround that has repeatedly proven difficult to sustain, leaving the troubled flag carrier once again searching for a stable path forward.

The return to a loss was a setback for a restructuring effort that had been positioned as central to stabilizing the airline and preparing it for a possible change of ownership. After a hard-won profit in the preceding period had offered a glimmer of progress, the renewed deficit underscored how fragile that improvement was and how exposed the carrier remains to cost pressures, debt and the volatility of its operating environment.

A turnaround that keeps slipping

SriLankan Airlines has spent years at the center of national policy debate, its finances entangled with the broader fiscal challenges of the Sri Lankan state. The restructuring program sought to address accumulated losses and a heavy debt burden, while opening the door to private investment that could relieve the public purse of an ongoing liability. Delays to that program by late 2025 suggested the process was proving more complex and slower than hoped.

For a relatively small flag carrier operating in a competitive regional market, sustaining profitability is a perennial struggle. Fuel costs, currency movements, aircraft financing and the need to invest in fleet and product all weigh heavily, and a single difficult year can erase the gains of a better one. The swing back to a loss illustrated exactly that dynamic.

A solitary profitable year does not make a turnaround; durability across cycles is the real test of whether a national carrier has been fixed.

Privatization back in focus

The renewed losses sharpened the debate over privatization, a recurring theme in discussions about the airline's future. Proponents argue that private capital and management discipline could deliver the consistent performance that public ownership has struggled to achieve, while shielding taxpayers from further support. Skeptics point to the difficulty of attracting investors to a carrier carrying legacy liabilities and operating in a tough market.

The pressures weighing on the carrier

  • A return to a net loss for the year ending March 2025 after a prior-year profit.
  • Delays to the government-led restructuring timeline.
  • Legacy debt and accumulated losses constraining flexibility.
  • Competitive regional dynamics and exposure to costs and currency swings.

Against this backdrop, the restructuring's stall left key questions unresolved: how quickly the program could regain momentum, whether a privatization transaction could be structured on terms acceptable to both the state and prospective investors, and how the airline would fund the investment needed to compete.

What it means

The renewed losses and stalled restructuring leave SriLankan Airlines at a familiar crossroads. The episode suggests that returning the carrier to durable profitability will require more than a single strong year, and that the privatization debate is unlikely to fade until a clear, executable plan emerges. For Sri Lanka, the stakes extend beyond the airline itself to questions of fiscal exposure and connectivity for an island economy that relies on air links for tourism and trade. The coming period will test whether the restructuring can be revived with enough conviction to attract investment, or whether the carrier remains caught in a cycle of partial fixes and recurring losses.
